title: Taxonomic Biomarker Discovery with LEfSe
author: Curtis Huttenhower
license_note: Confirmed via CrossRef DOI metadata: dx.doi.org/10.17504/protocols.io.d9699d

steps.title: Before beginning, we need to convert...
steps.instruction: Before beginning, we need to convert the sample names into consistent class names. This can easily be done by manually editing the output/merged_abundance_table.txt output/merged_abundance_table.txt generated in the previous protocols or using the following "sed" based Unix command: For this specific image we increased the LDA threshold to 4.7 in order to display a more compact image with fewer biomarkers. Notice that the cladograms produced by LEfSe can be less graphically appealing and detailed than those built using GraPhlAn . Using the tmp/merged_abundance_table.lefse.out file and, a combination of scripting or manual editing, it is possible to obtain an improved graphical output for LEfSe.

steps.title: The first LEfSe step consists of...
steps.instruction: The first LEfSe step consists of formatting the input table, making sure the class information is in the first row and scaling the values in [0,1M] which is useful for numerical computational reasons. $ format_input.py tmp/merged_abundance_table.4lefse.txt tmp/merged_abundance_table.lefse -c 1 -o 1000000

steps.title: Now, the LEfSe biomarker discovery tool...
steps.instruction: Now, the LEfSe biomarker discovery tool can be used with default statistical options. Here we change one default parameter to increase the threshold on the LDA effect size from 2 (default) to 4. $ run_lefse.py tmp/merged_abundance_table.lefse tmp/merged_abundance_table.lefse.out -l 4

steps.title: The results of the operation can...
steps.instruction: The results of the operation can now be displayed plotting the resulting list of biomarkers with corresponding effect sizes. $ plot_res.py --dpi 300 tmp/merged_abundance_table.lefse.out output_images/lefse_biomarkers.png

steps.title: The resulting image ( output_images/lefse_biomarkers
steps.instruction: The resulting image ( output_images/lefse_biomarkers.png output_images/lefse_biomarkers.png ) is shown below: a For this specific image we increased the LDA threshold to 4.7 in order to display a more compact image with fewer biomarkers.

steps.title: Another complementary visualization focuses on showing...
steps.instruction: Another complementary visualization focuses on showing the biomarkers on the underlying taxonomic tree. $ plot_cladogram.py --dpi 300 --format png tmp/merged_abundance_table.lefse.out output_images/lefse_biomarkers_cladogram.png

steps.title: The default plotting output is shown here
steps.instruction: The default plotting output is shown here: a Notice that the cladograms produced by LEfSe can be less graphically appealing and detailed than those built using GraPhlAn . Using the tmp/merged_abundance_table.lefse.out file and, a combination of scripting or manual editing, it is possible to obtain an improved graphical output for LEfSe.

steps.title: Single features can also be plotted as barplots with plot_features
steps.instruction: Single features can also be plotted as barplots with plot_features.py plot_features.py . For example, to plot the distribution of abundances for Firmicutes the command is: $ plot_features.py -f one --feature_name

steps.title: All features (or all biomarkers) can...
steps.instruction: All features (or all biomarkers) can also be exported in one compressed archive: Notice that with -f diff we are exporting only features that are biomarkers according to LEfSe, but with can export all features with -f all . $ plot_features.py -f diff --archive zip tmp/merged_abundance_table.lefse tmp/merged_abundance_table.lefse.out biomarkers.zip

steps.title: Here are the resulting figures for...
steps.instruction: Here are the resulting figures for Firmicutes and for Veillonellaceae (extracted from biomarkers.zip). a a
